summary: An old-school, transition-first 4-4-2 under Sergej Barbarez — attacking full-backs, talented wingers, two strikers, and a dual aerial threat for crosses into the box. Reached the finals via penalty-shootout wins over Wales and Italy and want more surprises.
style: Old-school 4-4-2 — play through the wide areas to the wingers (Memić, Bajraktarević, Alajbegović), with Džeko dropping to link, leading the line alongside Tabaković or Demirović. Direct and cross-heavy, aiming for the dual aerial threat in the box; transition over possession.

counterReliance.note: Transition over possession — direct, wide, and cross-heavy

strengths: Two big strikers as a direct aerial target in the box
Attacking full-backs and wingers combining to cross
Aggressive out of possession — most fouls of any of the 54 UEFA qualifying nations (17.7 per match)
Mental fortitude from back-to-back play-off shootout wins (Wales, Italy)
weaknesses: Among the lowest-possession sides — reliant on transition and set-piece/cross moments

keyPlayers.name: Edin Džeko
keyPlayers.note: All-time top scorer (73) and most-capped player; aerial target for long balls and crosses, can also drop to link

keyPlayers.name: Esmir Bajraktarević
keyPlayers.note: Direct dribbler with creativity and an eye for goal — 6 chances created & 8 successful dribbles vs Italy

tactics: Go direct to Džeko and the strikers, combine wide to cross into the box, and break in transition rather than build. They will happily foul to kill momentum. Group B offers a real chance to reach the knockouts for the first time — likely hinging on the final game vs Qatar.
notes: Since independence in 1992, Bosnia have reached just one World Cup (2014) and no Euros.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group B (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
